steps.title: Host environment
steps.hint: Where you sit and run your editor, terminal, and local builds.

options.label: Windows (native shell)
options.overview:
You work in PowerShell or CMD on Windows. Compilers and linkers run as native Windows
                processes. This is still the default for shipping Windows desktop binaries and for MSVC.

timeline.title: Visual C++ and Win32 SDK
timeline.summary: Developers used Visual Studio and the Platform SDK on Windows only.

timeline.title: Build Tools + cross-family awareness
timeline.summary: MSVC, MinGW, and Clang coexist; choosing one linker family per project matters.

alternates.title: Windows + WSL2
alternates.summary: Linux userland on Windows for apt packages and Linux-first scripts.
alternates.preferWhen: You need Linux packages or identical CI to Linux servers.
alternates.problemsSolved: Avoids dual-boot; keeps Windows shell for WinUI/MSVC workflows when needed.

options.label: Windows with WSL2
options.overview:
Windows 11 (and current Windows 10) run a real Linux kernel in WSL2. You get Linux
                toolchains and package managers while staying on a Windows machine. Path and debugger
                boundaries still matter when the *artifact* is a Windows .exe.

timeline.title: WSL1
timeline.summary: Syscall translation layer; fast filesystem on /mnt/c/ but incomplete Linux.

timeline.title: WSL2
timeline.summary: Full Linux VM integration; preferred for serious Linux development on Windows.

timeline.title: WSLg
timeline.summary: GUI Linux apps without a separate X server in many setups.

alternates.title: Native Windows only
alternates.summary: MSVC/MinGW on Windows without Linux VM overhead.
alternates.preferWhen: You ship Win32/WinUI and want the simplest debugging story.
alternates.problemsSolved: Fewer cross-filesystem path bugs; native debuggers attach directly.

options.label: Linux
options.overview: Native Linux host — typical for servers, containers, and cross-compilation targets.

options.label: macOS
options.overview: Apple toolchain (Xcode/clang) for macOS and often iOS targets.

options.label: Remote / SSH / cloud dev box
options.overview: Editor local; build and run on a remote machine or cloud environment.

steps.title: Target output
steps.hint: What you ship or run when the project is done.

options.label: Windows desktop app (Win32 / WinUI)
options.overview: GUI or desktop binaries using Win32, WinUI 3, or similar Windows-only APIs.

options.label: Windows CLI / service
options.overview: Console tools and Windows services; fewer GUI constraints than desktop apps.

options.label: Linux server / container image
options.overview: Deployable Linux binaries or OCI images.

options.label: Web (browser)
options.overview: Runtime in the browser; host OS matters less than Node/runtime in CI.

options.label: Help me choose
options.overview: Use the context panel and ranked guidance once other steps are filled in.

options.label: Microsoft MSVC (Visual Studio Build Tools)
options.overview:
Microsoft's C/C++ toolchain (cl/link/lib). Default for most Windows commercial libraries,
                WinUI, and Windows SDK samples. Produces MSVC-name-mangled binaries incompatible with MinGW.

timeline.title: Dominant Windows vendor toolchain
timeline.summary: Visual Studio and Build Tools ship cl.exe, link.exe, and the Windows SDK.

alternates.title: MinGW-w64 / MSYS2
alternates.summary: GCC on Windows; common in open source and cross-platform C++.
alternates.preferWhen: You want GCC, avoid full VS install, or depend on MinGW-built libs.
alternates.problemsSolved: Smaller install; familiar GCC flags; good for many CMake open-source projects.

alternates.title: Clang / clang-cl
alternates.summary: LLVM with optional MSVC-compatible driver on Windows.
alternates.preferWhen: You want LLVM diagnostics or sanizers with Windows SDK headers.
alternates.problemsSolved: Modern warnings and tooling; can target MSVC ABI with clang-cl.

options.label: MinGW-w64 / MSYS2 (GCC on Windows)
options.overview:
GNU toolchain on Windows, often via MSYS2. Popular for open-source C/C++ and for developers
                who prefer GCC. UCRT-based mingw-w64 is the modern default over legacy mingw32.

timeline.title: MinGW32
timeline.summary: Early GCC ports; msvcrt linkage quirks.

timeline.title: MinGW-w64 + UCRT
timeline.summary: 64-bit focus and UCRT alignment with modern Windows.

alternates.title: MSVC
alternates.summary: Vendor toolchain for Windows SDK and many proprietary SDKs.
alternates.preferWhen: You need WinUI, certain libs with only MSVC import libraries, or team standard is VS.
alternates.problemsSolved: Best support for latest Windows SDK features and vendor support contracts.

options.label: Clang / LLVM (incl. clang-cl on Windows)
options.overview: LLVM-based compilation; on Windows can use clang-cl to interoperate with MSVC libs.

options.label: GCC (native on Linux)
options.overview: Distro build-essential or cross gcc-* packages on Linux.

options.label: .NET SDK
options.overview: Managed compilation for C# and related languages.

options.label: rustup (Rust)
options.overview: Rust toolchains and cross targets via rustup.

steps.title: Build & run model
steps.hint: Where compilation happens vs where the binary runs.

options.label: Build and run on the host OS
options.overview: Simplest mental model: compiler OS matches target OS.

options.label: Build inside WSL; artifacts for Windows or Linux
options.overview:
Compile inside WSL2. For Linux targets, keep sources on the Linux filesystem. For Windows
                targets, install mingw-w64 inside WSL or prefer native Windows toolchain for debugging.

timeline.title: Cygwin / MSYS
timeline.summary: Unix-like layers on Windows before full Linux VM integration.

timeline.title: Linux build environment on Windows
timeline.summary: apt-installed toolchains; watch /mnt/c/ performance and path confusion.

options.label: Cross-compile on host (e.g. mingw on Linux)
options.overview: Linux or macOS host producing Windows binaries via mingw-w64 or LLVM-MinGW.

options.label: Build in container (Docker/Podman)
options.overview: Reproducible build image matching CI/production.

options.label: Local edit only; CI builds releases
options.overview: Laptop for editing; authoritative builds on CI runners.

recommendations.title: Cross-compile Windows binaries from Linux
recommendations.summary: Use a MinGW-w64 cross toolchain on Linux. Validate with Wine only for smoke tests; use Windows CI for release QA.
tooling: GitHub Actions windows-latest for release builds
caveats: Not all Windows APIs behave correctly under Wine

recommendations.title: Windows C++ on Windows + WSL2
recommendations.summary: For shipping Windows binaries, prefer native MSVC or MinGW on Windows unless you need Linux-only workflows. Use WSL2 for Linux targets and apt-based scripts.
tooling: Native: Visual Studio Build Tools + MSVC
WSL: mingw-w64 for Windows targets from Linux env
caveats: Path mixing across \\\\wsl$\\ and C:\\ confuses some tools
GUI debugging across WSL boundary is awkward

recommendations.title: Native Windows C++ with MSVC
recommendations.summary: Install Visual Studio Build Tools with Desktop development with C++.
caveats: MSVC object files are not link-compatible with MinGW

recommendations.title: Native Windows C++ with MinGW-w64
recommendations.summary: Install MSYS2 and the UCRT mingw-w64 toolchain.
caveats: Some proprietary libs ship MSVC import libs only

recommendations.title: Linux targets from Windows via WSL2
recommendations.summary: Use WSL2 with sources on the Linux filesystem; avoid heavy builds on /mnt/c/.
caveats: GUI needs WSLg or X server

recommendations.title: General guidance
recommendations.summary: Use the context panel for the selected option's history and alternates. Prefer the platform's official SDK for your target.
caveats: Cross-compilation trades convenience for debugging cost
